text:
CT ABDOMEN AND PELVIS WITH PANCREATIC PROTOCOL (TRIPHASIC)
Pre-Surgical Restaging Examination

CLINICAL INDICATION:
Mr. Raymond Kowalski is a 66-year-old male with biopsy-proven pancreatic head ductal adenocarcinoma (KRAS G12V/TP53/SMAD4-mutated, MSS). He was initially staged as borderline resectable (cT3N1M0) with SMA abutment and SMV/portal vein confluence involvement. He subsequently completed 8 cycles of neoadjuvant FOLFIRINOX followed by consolidative chemoradiation with concurrent capecitabine (50.4 Gy in 28 fractions over 5.5 weeks, completed approximately 6 weeks prior to this examination). This study is obtained for pre-surgical restaging to assess tumor response and resectability prior to planned pancreaticoduodenectomy. CA 19-9 most recently 62 U/mL (baseline 1200 U/mL). A biliary stent was placed at the time of initial diagnosis.

TECHNIQUE:
Multiphasic CT of the abdomen and pelvis was performed on a 128-slice multidetector CT scanner. Intravenous nonionic contrast material was administered, and images were acquired in the late arterial (pancreatic parenchymal), portal venous, and delayed phases. 3-mm axial sections reconstructed at 1-mm intervals with coronal and sagittal multiplanar reformations. Oral contrast was not administered. Dose modulation employed. Images reviewed on a dedicated workstation with multiplanar reformations.

COMPARISON:
CT abdomen/pelvis with pancreatic protocol following FOLFIRINOX cycle 8 (approximately 6 weeks prior). Prior CT following FOLFIRINOX cycle 4 and baseline CT at diagnosis also reviewed for longitudinal assessment.

FINDINGS:

PANCREAS:
There is a hypodense mass centered in the head of the pancreas measuring approximately 2.1 cm in greatest axial dimension (previously 2.4 cm on the post-FOLFIRINOX cycle 8 study, 3.1 cm at mid-treatment, and 3.8 cm at baseline). The lesion demonstrates heterogeneous internal attenuation with a central area of very low density consistent with treatment-related liquefactive necrosis or fibrotic involution, an appearance not present on prior examinations and strongly suggestive of multimodality treatment effect. The periphery of the mass retains some residual soft tissue density, though the overall conspicuity of the lesion has decreased compared to baseline. There is persistent upstream main pancreatic ductal dilation, now measuring approximately 5 mm in the neck and body (previously 6 mm at diagnosis), suggesting mild improvement in ductal obstruction. The pancreatic body and tail parenchyma appear atrophic in keeping with the chronic obstructive process.

VASCULAR RELATIONSHIPS:
Superior Mesenteric Artery (SMA): The fat plane between the mass and the right lateral wall of the proximal SMA appears largely reconstituted on this examination. There is minimal soft tissue density in immediate proximity to the SMA, and estimated contact, if any, involves less than 60 degrees of the vessel's circumference — representing a progressive and substantial improvement from 120-150 degrees at baseline and approximately 90 degrees on the prior post-FOLFIRINOX study. No encasement or arterial irregularity is identified.

Superior Mesenteric Vein and Portal Vein Confluence (SMV/PV): The SMV-portal vein confluence appears patent with preserved luminal caliber and no thrombus. The previously described short-segment contact between the mass and the SMV wall appears diminished, with a thin fat plane now perceptible along the majority of the vessel interface. No definite venous encasement is identified, though close proximity persists at one small segment. The main portal vein, splenic vein, and distal SMV are patent.

Celiac Axis: No involvement. Fat plane preserved.

Common Hepatic Artery: No periartrial soft tissue or encasement. Patent.

BILIARY SYSTEM:
A biliary stent remains in place within the common bile duct, appearing unchanged in position. The intrahepatic bile ducts are not dilated. The common bile duct measures approximately 8 mm in caliber at the level of the stent, within acceptable limits given stent presence. No intraductal stones. The gallbladder is not distended and demonstrates no wall thickening or pericholecystic fluid.

LIVER:
The hepatic parenchyma is homogeneous in attenuation on all phases without evidence of focal hepatic lesions. No hypervascular or hypovascular nodules are identified. No capsular retraction. No biliary ductal dilation. The hepatic veins and inferior vena cava are patent. No perihepatic fluid.

LYMPH NODES:
The previously identified enlarged periportal lymph nodes are no longer appreciably enlarged; the dominant node previously measuring 1.2 cm at baseline, reduced to 0.8 cm at mid-treatment, and 0.6 cm post-FOLFIRINOX cycle 8, is not clearly identifiable as a discrete enlarged node on the current examination. No peripancreatic, celiac, portocaval, para-aortic, or mesenteric lymph nodes meeting size criteria for pathologic enlargement are identified. Lymph nodes within the surgical field appear within normal limits.

PERITONEUM AND MESENTERY:
No peritoneal implants, nodular peritoneal thickening, or omental caking identified. No ascites. Mild mesenteric fat haziness in the right upper quadrant likely reflects residual change from prior radiation therapy to this region.

SPLEEN:
Spleen is normal in size (approximately 10.2 cm craniocaudal dimension), homogeneous, without focal lesions or perisplenic fluid.

ADRENAL GLANDS:
Bilateral adrenal glands are of normal morphology and attenuation. No adrenal nodules.

KIDNEYS AND URETERS:
Bilateral kidneys are of normal size and enhancement pattern without hydronephrosis, masses, or calculi. No ureteral abnormality. Renal cortical thinning is symmetric and mild, consistent with patient's age and known hypertension.

AORTA AND MAJOR VESSELS:
The abdominal aorta is mildly ectatic but without aneurysm or significant stenosis. Atherosclerotic calcifications noted along the aortic wall and mesenteric vessels. No dissection.

BONY STRUCTURES:
No lytic or blastic osseous lesions suspicious for metastatic involvement. Degenerative changes of the lumbar spine noted at multiple levels, compatible with age.

SOFT TISSUES:
No soft tissue masses. No hernia. Mild subcutaneous tissue reduction noted compared to prior studies, consistent with the documented weight loss during treatment course.

IMPRESSION:

1. Continued interval reduction of the pancreatic head mass, now measuring approximately 2.1 cm (from 2.4 cm after FOLFIRINOX cycle 8, and 3.8 cm at baseline), reflecting ongoing response to the combined neoadjuvant chemotherapy and consolidative chemoradiation program. The prominent central hypodensity with surrounding fibrotic tissue is a morphologic pattern characteristic of treatment-related necrosis and favorable tumor response.

2. Favorable evolution of vascular relationships. SMA fat plane appears largely reconstituted with greatly reduced tumoral contact, estimated at less than 60 degrees if any residual contact remains. SMV-portal vein confluence is patent and shows reduced tumor-vessel interface compared to prior examinations. These vascular findings are substantially improved compared to the baseline borderline resectable configuration and are consistent with potential surgical downstaging.

3. Resolution of previously identified suspicious periportal lymphadenopathy. No lymph nodes meeting size criteria for pathologic enlargement are currently identified in the surgical field or at regional nodal stations.

4. No evidence of hepatic metastases, peritoneal carcinomatosis, or new distant disease.

5. Biliary stent in stable position. No biliary obstruction.

Overall, the imaging appearances are consistent with an ongoing and meaningful response to multimodality neoadjuvant therapy. Based on the current vascular anatomy, the tumor appears potentially amenable to surgical resection with intent for negative margins, though intraoperative assessment of the SMV interface will remain important.
